raw_case:
Solitary metastasis to the head of the pancreas from lung adenocarcinoma mimicking pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma: A case report
Solitary pancreatic metastasis is a rare cause of pancreatic neoplasm. Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma is the primary differential consideration when a solitary pancreatic mass is diagnosed, as it is the most common solitary solid pancreatic neoplasm. A majority of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinomas arise in the region of the head of the pancreas; however, specific neoplastic and non-neoplastic lesions can occur at or adjacent to the pancreatic head, which can mimic a pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Therefore, a histopathological diagnosis is essential for confirming pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Isolated solitary metastasis from primary lung adenocarcinoma is a rare cause of a solitary pancreatic head mass. We report a case in which imaging and pathology were integral to the diagnosis of a solitary lung adenocarcinoma metastasis to the head of the pancreas, which ultimately guided appropriate patient management.

Introduction

Metastases to the pancreas are an uncommon cause of a neoplastic process involving the pancreas [1]. Renal cell cancer is the most common cause of metastatic disease to the pancreas, followed by lung cancer [2]. However, it should be noted that the pancreas is not a common site of metastasis from lung cancer. Lung cancer commonly metastasizes outside the thoracic cavity to the liver (34%), adrenal glands (33%), bones (15%), central nervous system (12%), and kidneys (11%). Only about 5% of metastatic lung cancers spread to the pancreas [3]. Among lung cancers, metastases from small cell lung cancer, the most common cause of pancreatic metastases, are 5 times more common than metastases from lung adenocarcinoma [4]. A multiplicity of lesions favors metastases as an etiology. However, when there is a solitary pancreatic lesion in the absence of metastases elsewhere, especially in the setting of no metastatic disease on prior imaging work-up of a known malignancy, it raises a concern for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. We report a rare case where there was a solitary pancreatic head metastasis from lung adenocarcinoma mimicking a pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.

Case report

A 74-year-old female patient with a history of hypertension and lung cancer presented to her primary care physician with painless jaundice. Three months prior, the patient had undergone left anterolateral thoracotomy and extended left lower lobe lobectomy for stage II lung cancer with final postsurgical pathology consistent with a pT2bN0 poorly differentiated lung adenocarcinoma. Upon subsequent presentation with painless jaundice, she underwent an endoscopic ultrasound, which showed a 1.8 cm hypoechoic pancreatic head mass obstructing the common bile duct, prompting an endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration. Cytology revealed malignant cells consistent with adenocarcinoma.Postendoscopic ultrasound, a fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) positron emission tomography-computed tomography (PET-CT) was performed for further workup at an outside hospital. It demonstrated an FDG avid pancreatic head mass (Fig. 1) and no other areas of abnormal FDG uptake. Of note, 4 months prior, a PET-CT performed for metastatic workup before the resection of the lung adenocarcinoma was negative for metastatic disease (Fig. 1), and a presumptive diagnosis of primary pancreatic adenocarcinoma was made. The patient was then referred to our institution for staging and evaluation for Whipple's procedure. Abdominal MRI was performed as a part of the staging work-up and demonstrated a 2.2 cm well-circumscribed, T2 hyperintense, and heterogeneously enhancing pancreatic head mass, which was hypoenhancing when compared to the normal pancreatic parenchyma (Figs. 2A-D). Associated dilatation of the common bile duct and the main pancreatic duct (double duct sign) was present. Given the mass demonstrating hypoenhancement on the arterial phase when compared to the normal pancreatic parenchyma with associated dilatation of the main pancreatic duct and common bile duct, a primary differential consideration of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma was considered as it is the most likely differential of a pancreatic head mass specifically in the setting of a hypoenhancing mass. The findings that were against the diagnosis of a pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma were that the mass appeared well delineated without infiltrative margins, contrary to the infiltrative nature of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma and the fact that the patient had a prior history of lung adenocarcinoma, and the histopathology of the pancreatic mass raised of the possibility of an adenocarcinoma [5].Given MRI features, the histopathology slides were re-examined at our institution and tested for pancreatic and lung adenocarcinoma immunohistochemical markers. The neoplastic cells were focally positive for CK7 and TTF-1 markers, which are markers of lung adenocarcinoma, and were negative for CA19-9, CK20, and p53, which are markers for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, favoring lung adenocarcinoma metastasis. This led to appropriate patient management, preventing unnecessary surgical intervention in a metastatic cancer patient who would otherwise have undergone a Whipple's procedure, which is the standard of care for a resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma. The patient was managed medically, and the 1-year follow-up CT demonstrated a good treatment response to chemotherapy and immunotherapy, with a marked interval decrease in the tumor size (Figs. 2E and F). The timeline of events is described in detail in Fig. 3.

Discussion

Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma is the most common solid pancreatic mass, constituting more significant than 85% of the solid pancreatic neoplasms [6]. Pancreatic adenocarcinoma is most commonly seen in the pancreatic head, with about 65% of the tumors arising in the head region [7]. Imaging is integral in the diagnosis of a pancreatic head mass. CT and MRI are equally accurate in diagnosing and staging pancreatic head masses [8].Typically, pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma involving the head region presents as an ill-defined infiltrative hypoenhancing mass on computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). It is best appreciated in the arterial phase of contrast-enhanced CT/MRI. Additionally, due to the malignancy's infiltrative nature, it can cause ductal obstructions and is associated with upstream dilatation of the common bile duct and the main pancreatic duct, commonly known as the double duct sign. Distal pancreatic atrophy can also be associated. There is a propensity for vascular encasement and perineural spread [5,6,9]. Pathological masses that arise in the pancreas can be grouped into hypoenhancing/isoenhancing versus hyperenhancing masses, comparing the enhancement of the mass with the enhancement of the background pancreatic parenchyma. If there is a focal isoenhancing/hypoenhancing pancreatic head mass, pancreatic adenocarcinoma is the primary differential consideration [5,10].Neoplastic etiologies in the pancreatic head region that mimic a pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma include hypoenhancing masses arising in the pancreas, or masses adjacent to the pancreas which closely abut or infiltrate the pancreatic head. Masses in the pancreatic head include uncommon primary pancreatic head neoplasms, lymphoma, and solitary pancreatic head metastasis. Metastatic lymphadenopathy in the periportal region can closely abut the head of the pancreas and mimic a mass arising from the pancreas. Other neoplastic etiologies arising adjacent to the pancreas include cholangiocarcinoma involving the terminal common bile duct and ampullary adenocarcinoma. Non-neoplastic etiologies that can mimic pancreatic ductal carcinoma include a mass forming chronic pancreatitis, groove pancreatitis, and autoimmune pancreatitis [5]. Hyper-enhancing pancreatic masses include pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors and hypervascular metastasis, most commonly neuroendocrine, renal, and melanoma metastasis [5,11]. The differential diagnoses of a solitary pancreatic head mass are summarized in Fig. 4.Metastatic disease of the pancreas constitutes a minuscule proportion (2%) of pancreatic malignancies. Metastatic involvement can be in the form of multiple pancreatic lesions, which can be seen in about 5% to 10% of the patients, as diffuse infiltration of the pancreas accounting for about 15%-44% of the patients, and as a solitary, isolated mass seen in about 50%-75% of the patients. Renal cell cancer accounts for more than 60% of pancreatic metastases, followed by lung cancer and melanoma [2]. Other primary malignancies metastasizing to the pancreas include cancers of the breast and colon and metastatic sarcoma [12]. Metastatic disease of the pancreas can be asymptomatic, with diagnosis incidentally found on imaging as part of metastatic work-up. If the metastatic disease involves the pancreatic head region, it can present as obstructive jaundice. Rarely, it can present as acute pancreatitis caused as a result of obstruction of the main pancreatic duct [2].Metastatic lesions to the pancreas demonstrate a round or ovoid morphology and appear relatively well-marginated. Infiltration along the pancreatic vasculature is less common, unlike primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma [13]. Metastasis to the pancreas may be broadly classified into hypoenhancing and hyperenhancing lesions. Solitary hypoenhancing pancreatic metastasis is a differential consideration for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, the most common hypoenhancing pancreatic mass. Hypovascular pancreatic metastases include metastasis from the lung, breast, and colorectal cancer metastasis [5,14]. The primary differential consideration for a hyperenhancing pancreatic mass is a neuroendocrine tumor. Solitary pancreatic metastasis from renal cell cancer, melanoma, and neuroendocrine tumors are differential considerations for a solitary hyperenhancing pancreatic mass [5,11].Common sites for extrathoracic lung cancer metastases include liver, brain, adrenals, and bones. Pancreatic metastases from lung cancer are rare, with an incidence quoted between 5% and 11% in the literature [4]. The incidence varies according to the histological subtype, with small cell lung cancer being the most common to metastasize to the pancreas (12%), followed by adenocarcinoma being the second most common (2.4%), squamous cell (1.9%), and then large cell (1.1%) [4]. A retrospective study on pancreatic metastases from lung cancer done by Maneo et al. showed that the most typical pattern of pancreatic involvement is a solitary pancreatic mass (73.1%), followed by diffuse involvement (15.4%) and multiple pancreatic masses (11.4%) [15]. As metastatic spread to the pancreas is usually seen in advanced lung cancer, prognosis is generally poor, with surgical intervention being rare and treatment primarily involving radiotherapy and/or chemotherapy [16].Shadhu et al. [1] reported a similar case where there was a metastasis to the pancreatic tail after 2 years of resection of the primary lung adenocarcinoma. In their case, the diagnosis was made on histopathology postsurgical resection. Akpoviroro et al. [4] reported a case where there was isolated pancreatic metastasis in the setting of a patient undergoing chemoradiotherapy for inoperable lung adenocarcinoma. Zhou et al. [17] reported a case of an isolated pancreatic metastasis from a squamous cell lung carcinoma mimicking a primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Previous studies have shown that patients with metastatic pancreatic malignancies originating from a lung primary typically have a median latency of 20 months and an average latency of 21.9 months [16]. Our patient was diagnosed with biopsy-proven primary lung adenocarcinoma in March 2023. Following a left lower lobectomy with en bloc resection in April, the patient presented in June with metastasis, which occurred just over 3 months from the primary diagnosis—an unusually short timeline. A study by Yoon et al. [18] found that less than 0.28% of metastatic pancreatic lesions are associated with non-small cell lung carcinoma. The early latency and less than 1 percent occurrence of this patient's metastatic pancreatic malignancy makes our case unique.

Clinical significance

It is necessary to differentiate pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma from the other neoplastic and non-neoplastic etiologies of a solitary hypoenhancing pancreatic head mass, as the appropriate management depends on the accurate diagnosis of the etiology. A histopathological confirmation is therefore crucial to confirm the diagnosis of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma before further management [5,11]. Tissue sampling can be performed either through endoscopic ultrasound-guided or percutaneous tissue sampling. Whipple's procedure is performed for operable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma and other neoplastic etiologies and non-neoplastic pathologies of the pancreatic head for which surgery is indicated, while it may or may not be the treatment for choice for different etiologies of a solitary pancreatic head mass [11].

Limitations

Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor are the most common hypoenhancing and hyperenhancing solitary pancreatic masses, respectively. Imaging helps in narrowing the differential diagnosis. However, if a solitary pancreatic mass is managed based on imaging alone, there is a possibility of mismanagement. A multidisciplinary approach by the radiology, pathology, gastroenterology, and surgery/surgical oncology teams is essential in patient management.

Conclusion

Although pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma is the most common solid pancreatic tumor, other neoplastic and non-neoplastic processes can occur in the pancreas, which can mimic a pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Imaging and pathology are vital in the diagnosis and appropriate patient management. The learning points from the present case report are a) Although metastatic disease is a rare cause of a solitary pancreatic mass, it should be included in the differential in the setting of a known primary malignancy, b) Isolated solitary pancreatic metastasis can occur in the absence of metastatic disease elsewhere in the body, and c) Pancreatic metastatic disease can occur even though the primary malignancy appears to be well controlled/satisfactorily treated.

Patient consent

The subject has been acknowledged and approved by the IRB.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 74-year-old female  
- **Chief Complaint:** Painless jaundice  
- **History of Present Illness:** Presented with painless jaundice three months after undergoing left anterolateral thoracotomy and extended left lower lobectomy for stage II lung adenocarcinoma. Imaging revealed a solitary pancreatic head mass.  
- **Past Medical History:** Stage II lung adenocarcinoma (poorly differentiated, pT2bN0) treated with lobectomy.  
- **Personal History:** Hypertension  
- **Family History:** None mentioned  
- **Physical Examination:** None mentioned  
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - **Endoscopic ultrasound:** 1.8 cm hypoechoic pancreatic head mass obstructing the common bile duct.  
  - **Cytology:** Malignant cells consistent with adenocarcinoma.  
  - **Initial PET-CT:** FDG-avid pancreatic head mass with no other areas of abnormal uptake (prior PET-CT was negative for metastasis).  
  - **Abdominal MRI:** 2.2 cm well-circumscribed, T2 hyperintense, heterogeneously enhancing pancreatic head mass with double duct sign; hypoenhancing compared to pancreatic parenchyma.  
  - **Immunohistochemistry:** CK7 and TTF-1 (markers for lung adenocarcinoma) positive; CA19-9, CK20, and p53 (markers for pancreatic adenocarcinoma) negative.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial differential diagnoses based on the solitary pancreatic head mass**:  
   - Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC): Common etiology of solitary pancreatic masses, especially in hypoenhancing lesions with associated bile and duct obstruction (double duct sign).  
   - Pancreatic metastasis: Less common cause but should be considered in the context of prior malignancy.  

2. **Ruling out pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma:**  
   - **Supporting features for PDAC:** Hypoenhancing mass, location in the pancreatic head, and bile/pancreatic duct obstruction.  
   - **Against PDAC:** Well-circumscribed margins, absence of infiltrative features, patient's history of lung adenocarcinoma, and immunohistochemical markers being negative for pancreatic adenocarcinoma.  

3. **Confirming pancreatic metastasis from lung adenocarcinoma:**  
   - Immunohistochemical testing was crucial. Positive CK7 and TTF-1 confirmed lung origin, while negative markers for pancreatic adenocarcinoma excluded PDAC.  

4. **Excluding other etiologies for a solitary pancreatic mass:**  
   - Other rare primary pancreatic lesions (neuroendocrine tumors, lymphoma, etc.) were not consistent with imaging or clinical data.  
   - Non-neoplastic causes like chronic pancreatitis or autoimmune pancreatitis were also inconsistent with histopathology and imaging findings.  

Thus, the final diagnosis of metastatic lung adenocarcinoma to the pancreatic head was reached.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Solitary pancreatic metastasis from lung adenocarcinoma, mimicking pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. The diagnosis was established through a combination of imaging, histopathology, and immunohistochemical testing, which guided appropriate non-surgical management.

results.input:
Please carefully study the following patient case summary, conduct a comprehensive and in-depth diagnostic analysis, and clearly provide the final diagnosis result.

- **Patient Information:** 74-year-old female  
- **Chief Complaint:** Painless jaundice  
- **History of Present Illness:** Presented with painless jaundice three months after undergoing left anterolateral thoracotomy and extended left lower lobectomy for stage II lung adenocarcinoma. Imaging revealed a solitary pancreatic head mass.  
- **Past Medical History:** Stage II lung adenocarcinoma (poorly differentiated, pT2bN0) treated with lobectomy.  
- **Personal History:** Hypertension  
- **Family History:** None mentioned  
- **Physical Examination:** None mentioned  
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - **Endoscopic ultrasound:** 1.8 cm hypoechoic pancreatic head mass obstructing the common bile duct.  
  - **Cytology:** Malignant cells consistent with adenocarcinoma.  
  - **Initial PET-CT:** FDG-avid pancreatic head mass with no other areas of abnormal uptake (prior PET-CT was negative for metastasis).  
  - **Abdominal MRI:** 2.2 cm well-circumscribed, T2 hyperintense, heterogeneously enhancing pancreatic head mass with double duct sign; hypoenhancing compared to pancreatic parenchyma.  
  - **Immunohistochemistry:** CK7 and TTF-1 (markers for lung adenocarcinoma) positive; CA19-9, CK20, and p53 (markers for pancreatic adenocarcinoma) negative.

---

**Format to Follow:**

```
### Resoning:
[Please sort out your thinking process step by step, with each logical step in a separate paragraph.]
<step 1> Specific thinking content of this step
<step 2> Specific thinking content of this step
...
<step n> Specific thinking content of this step

### Answer:
[Just output the diagnostic result without any other explanation.]
```
results.content:
### Reasoning:
The patient presents with painless jaundice, a clinical sign highly suggestive of biliary obstruction, often caused by a mass in the pancreatic head. Imaging confirms a pancreatic head mass that is causing obstruction of the common bile duct, leading to jaundice. Cytology of the mass has confirmed it to be adenocarcinoma.

The patient has a significant past medical history of stage II lung adenocarcinoma, treated with lobectomy. The current pancreatic head mass was identified three months after this surgery, raising the suspicion of either a new primary pancreatic cancer or a metastasis from the lung primary.

While the location (pancreatic head) and initial presentation (jaundice) are classic for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, the immunohistochemistry (IHC) results provide crucial differentiating information. The mass is positive for CK7 and TTF-1. TTF-1 is a transcription factor highly specific for lung adenocarcinoma (and thyroid carcinoma, which is not a consideration here given the clinical context). CK7 is also typically positive in lung adenocarcinomas. Conversely, the mass is negative for CA19-9, CK20, and p53. While CA19-9 is a common tumor marker for pancreatic cancer, it can be negative. However, the combination of TTF-1 positivity and CK7 positivity, along with negativity for other markers, strongly points towards the tumor originating from the lung. Primary pancreatic adenocarcinomas are typically CK7 positive and TTF-1 negative.

Therefore, synthesizing the clinical presentation, imaging findings, cytological confirmation of adenocarcinoma, and critically, the definitive immunohistochemical profile, the pancreatic head mass is best classified as a metastatic lesion originating from the patient's known lung adenocarcinoma. The absence of other metastatic lesions on the initial PET-CT scan does not exclude a solitary metastasis to the pancreas, especially given the recent history of lung cancer.

### Answer:
Metastatic lung adenocarcinoma to the pancreas

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with painless jaundice, a clinical sign highly suggestive of biliary obstruction, often caused by a mass in the pancreatic head. Imaging confirms a pancreatic head mass that is causing obstruction of the common bile duct, leading to jaundice. Cytology of the mass has confirmed it to be adenocarcinoma.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: Painless jaundice，Biliary obstruction，Pancreatic head mass，Common bile duct obstruction，Pancreatic adenocarcinoma，Cytology diagnosis
judgment_path.info:
Painless jaundice is a key symptom of malignant biliary obstruction, primarily caused by pancreatic adenocarcinoma. Patients often present at advanced stages, which limits curative treatment options. Timely diagnosis typically relies on imaging and clinical evaluation to confirm bile duct obstruction, with endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) being central to intervention. Challenges in the diagnosis include the need for effective biomarkers for early detection, as current options like CA 19-9 lack specificity. Managing biliary obstruction involves careful decision-making regarding preoperative biliary drainage to relieve jaundice and prevent complications from chemotherapy, while balancing the risks of procedures like pancreatitis. Endoscopic techniques, particularly using metal stents, have shown improved outcomes over plastic stents, and advancements in ERCP methods, including EUS-assisted techniques, enhance access and reduce complications in complex cases.

Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) often presents with subtle, nonspecific symptoms, particularly in elderly patients with multiple comorbidities, leading to late diagnosis. Key clinical indicators include progressive unintentional weight loss, early satiety, and painless jaundice due to biliary obstruction, with the "double-duct sign" on imaging (simultaneous dilation of the common bile duct and main pancreatic duct) being a strong radiologic marker for malignancy. Diagnosis is confirmed through laboratory tests demonstrating elevated tumor markers like CA 19-9, imaging studies, and endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine-needle biopsy. Multidisciplinary management is essential for care coordination, including symptom management, nutritional support, and consideration of patient-centered goals, particularly in frail elderly individuals with complex health profiles.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient has a significant past medical history of stage II lung adenocarcinoma, treated with lobectomy. The current pancreatic head mass was identified three months after this surgery, raising the suspicion of either a new primary pancreatic cancer or a metastasis from the lung primary.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: lung adenocarcinoma stage II，lobectomy，pancreatic head mass，pancreatic cancer differential diagnosis，lung cancer metastasis to pancreas
judgment_path.info: Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) symptoms can vary but may include persistent cough, chest pain, shortness of breath, and unexplained weight loss. Diagnosis often involves imaging tests and biopsies to stage the cancer, which is crucial for determining treatment options. Treatment is tailored to the cancer stage, including surgery for early stages (0 and I), potentially followed by adjuvant therapies like chemotherapy or immunotherapy for high-risk patients. For stage II and III, neoadjuvant therapy and chemoradiation may precede surgery, with options for advanced stages (IVA and IVB) focusing on immunotherapy, targeted therapy, or palliative care due to the complexity and spread of the disease. Understanding the cancer's characteristics and the patient's overall health is essential in forming an effective treatment plan.

reasoning_eval.step: While the location (pancreatic head) and initial presentation (jaundice) are classic for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, the immunohistochemistry (IHC) results provide crucial differentiating information. The mass is positive for CK7 and TTF-1. TTF-1 is a transcription factor highly specific for lung adenocarcinoma (and thyroid carcinoma, which is not a consideration here given the clinical context). CK7 is also typically positive in lung adenocarcinomas. Conversely, the mass is negative for CA19-9, CK20, and p53. While CA19-9 is a common tumor marker for pancreatic cancer, it can be negative. However, the combination of TTF-1 positivity and CK7 positivity, along with negativity for other markers, strongly points towards the tumor originating from the lung. Primary pancreatic adenocarcinomas are typically CK7 positive and TTF-1 negative.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma，Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma，Jaundice，Lung adenocarcinoma，Thyroid carcinoma，TTF-1 marker，CK7 marker，CA19-9 marker，CK20 marker，p53 marker，Immunohistochemistry，Tumor marker
judgment_path.info:
Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is associated with significant challenges in both diagnosis and treatment, leading to a generally poor prognosis compared to other cancers. Symptoms may include abdominal pain, weight loss, and jaundice, although many patients remain asymptomatic in early stages. Diagnosis typically involves imaging techniques such as cross-sectional imaging and biopsy for histological confirmation. Staging categorizes PDAC as resectable, borderline resectable, or locally advanced, influencing treatment approaches, which often consist of multimodal therapies including chemotherapy and surgical interventions. Notably, the use of tumor markers and ongoing adaptation of treatment strategies based on radiological and biomarker responses are essential for optimal patient management.

Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is an aggressive cancer with key symptoms including weakness, loss of appetite, weight loss, abdominal pain, jaundice, and nausea. It is challenging to diagnose as presenting symptoms are non-specific and usually require further investigation. Diagnosis typically involves laboratory tests, such as liver function tests and the tumor marker CA 19-9, alongside imaging studies like abdominal CT, MRI, or endoscopic ultrasound for mass identification. Early detection is rare, with many cases diagnosed at advanced stages, leading to poor prognosis: overall 5-year survival is around 7.2%, with surgical resection being the only potential cure.

Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is a highly aggressive cancer with a poor prognosis and rising incidence, primarily due to late diagnosis. Common symptoms may include jaundice, weight loss, and abdominal or back pain, often appearing at advanced stages. Diagnostic techniques include imaging (CT, MRI, EUS) and tumor markers like CA19-9, but detection remains challenging due to the pancreas's deep location and vague early symptoms. More advanced molecular profiling and liquid biopsies are being explored to improve early diagnosis and monitor treatment responses. Unfortunately, only a small fraction of patients present with resectable disease at diagnosis, and treatment options, particularly for metastatic cases, are limited, focusing largely on palliative care. New therapeutic approaches, including combination therapies and targeted treatments based on genetic profiling, are under investigation to enhance outcomes for patients with PDAC.

reasoning_eval.step: Therefore, synthesizing the clinical presentation, imaging findings, cytological confirmation of adenocarcinoma, and critically, the definitive immunohistochemical profile, the pancreatic head mass is best classified as a metastatic lesion originating from the patient's known lung adenocarcinoma. The absence of other metastatic lesions on the initial PET-CT scan does not exclude a solitary metastasis to the pancreas, especially given the recent history of lung cancer.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: Pancreatic head mass，Metastatic lesion，Lung adenocarcinoma，Immunohistochemical profile，Solitary metastasis，PET-CT scan，Clinical presentation，Imaging findings，Cytological confirmation
judgment_path.info:
This content discusses pancreatic conditions, particularly focusing on the diagnosis and symptoms of pancreatic cancer. Key symptoms include late-stage diagnosis and severe outcomes, with the disease often being fatal. Diagnostic methods mentioned include spiral CT scans to identify pancreatic carcinoma and the importance of monitoring postoperative abdominal fluid for diagnosing pancreatic fistulas. Additionally, conditions like pancreatic tuberculosis and abscesses require specific diagnostic attention and treatment interventions, such as drainage for abscesses.



Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is a highly aggressive form of cancer with a poor prognosis, as it often presents at advanced stages, rendering curative surgery infeasible. Key symptoms include weight loss, abdominal pain, and jaundice, but diagnosis is challenging due to the lack of specific early signs. PDAC is predominantly linked to mutations in the KRAS oncogene, observed in approximately 90% of cases, contributing to tumor initiation and progression. Additionally, risk factors such as diet-induced obesity and chronic stress are associated with increased disease severity and poor outcomes. Current diagnostic methods fail to detect early pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PanIN) lesions, necessitating advancements for earlier detection. Emerging therapeutic approaches focus on novel drugs targeting KRAS and its signaling pathways, alongside strategies to modify risk factors related to diet and stress, with the aim of improving patient prognosis.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> The initial differential diagnoses for a solitary pancreatic head mass included pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) and pancreatic metastasis, with PDAC being the more common etiology, especially in hypoenhancing lesions with associated bile and duct obstruction, while pancreatic metastasis was considered less common but relevant in the context of prior malignancy.

<Step 2> Features supporting PDAC included the hypoenhancing mass, its location in the pancreatic head, and bile/pancreatic duct obstruction, while features against PDAC included the well-circumscribed margins, absence of infiltrative features, the patient's history of lung adenocarcinoma, and negative immunohistochemical markers for pancreatic adenocarcinoma.

<Step 3> Positive CK7 and TTF-1 immunohistochemical testing confirmed lung adenocarcinoma as the origin, and negative markers for pancreatic adenocarcinoma excluded PDAC, thereby confirming pancreatic metastasis from lung adenocarcinoma.
<Step 4> Other rare primary pancreatic lesions, such as neuroendocrine tumors or lymphoma, were excluded because they were inconsistent with imaging findings and clinical data.
<Step 5> Non-neoplastic causes, including chronic pancreatitis and autoimmune pancreatitis, were excluded based on inconsistency with histopathology and imaging findings.

<Step 6> The final diagnosis was solitary pancreatic metastasis from lung adenocarcinoma, mimicking pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, established through imaging, histopathology, and immunohistochemical testing.
<Step 7> The diagnosis guided an appropriate non-surgical management strategy.
